title: Carrier Transport & Mobility
description: Animate electron and hole motion under electric fields. See drift velocity, diffusion, the Einstein relation, and how doping and temperature affect mobility.

steps.instruction: At E = 0 V/cm, both electrons (blue) and holes (red circles) move randomly — this is thermal diffusion. The random walk has no net direction.
steps.hint: Thermal energy kT gives each carrier an average kinetic energy of (3/2)kT. The random motion averages out to zero net current.

steps.instruction: Set E = +200 V/cm. Electrons drift left (opposite to E), holes drift right (with E). The arrows on the canvas show the field direction.
steps.hint: Drift current density: Jn = q·n·μn·E (electrons), Jp = q·p·μp·E (holes). Both contribute to current in the SAME direction despite carriers moving in opposite directions.

steps.instruction: Observe the mobility values in the metrics panel. At Nd = 10^16 cm⁻³, μn ≈ 1200 cm²/V·s and μp ≈ 450 cm²/V·s. Increase doping to 10^18 cm⁻³ — both mobilities drop due to ionised impurity scattering.
steps.hint: Mobility decreases at high doping because more charged impurities scatter the carriers. This is described by the Caughey-Thomas model.

steps.instruction: Increase temperature from 300 K to 450 K. Mobility decreases (lattice scattering dominates at high T). At very low temperatures, mobility would increase (fewer phonons).
steps.hint: Phonon scattering: μ ∝ T^(-3/2). Impurity scattering: μ ∝ T^(+3/2). Total mobility is determined by Matthiessen's rule: 1/μ = 1/μphonon + 1/μimpurity.

steps.instruction: The diffusion coefficient D is shown below the mobility. Notice D/μ = kT/q — this is the Einstein relation. At 300 K, D/μ = 0.02585 V (the thermal voltage).
steps.hint: The Einstein relation links diffusion to drift — both stem from the same carrier scattering physics. It's fundamental to the design of bipolar transistors and diodes.
